question_text: When the profit-sharing ratio changes and goodwill is raised and immediately written off, the journal entries are:
explanation: When goodwill is raised and immediately written off: Entry 1 (Raise): Dr. Goodwill A/c, Cr. All Partners' Capital A/cs (OLD ratio). Entry 2 (Write off): Dr. All Partners' Capital A/cs (NEW ratio), Cr. Goodwill A/c. The net effect automatically adjusts capital accounts — the gaining partner's capital decreases and the sacrificing partner's capital increases, which is the desired compensation.
topic: Goodwill Treatment on Change in PSR
subject: accountancy

options.text: Raised in new ratio, written off in old ratio
Raised in sacrificing ratio, written off in gaining ratio
Raised in old ratio, written off in new ratio
Raised and written off both in old ratio

elimination_hints.hint: Raising goodwill reflects the period when it was built (old ratio). Writing off distributes it to the new stakeholders (new ratio). Reversing this defeats the purpose.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the ratios — using new for raising and old for writing off.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrificing/gaining ratio is used for the single-entry method (direct adjustment). When actually raising and writing off goodwill, old and new ratios are used.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direct goodwill adjustment method with the raise-and-write-off method.

elimination_hints.hint: If both raised and written off in old ratio, the entries cancel each other completely, resulting in zero adjustment — defeating the purpose.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the same ratio for both entries, which produces no net effect.

question_text: A, B and C share profits in 2:2:1. They agree to share in 3:1:1. Goodwill is valued at ₹50,000. If goodwill is adjusted through a single entry (without raising in books), the entry will be:
explanation:
Old: A=2/5, B=2/5, C=1/5. New: A=3/5, B=1/5, C=1/5.
Gain/Sacrifice: A = 3/5−2/5 = +1/5 (gained). B = 1/5−2/5 = −1/5 (sacrificed). C = 1/5−1/5 = 0.
A (gainer) compensates B (sacrificer). Amount = 50,000 × 1/5 = ₹10,000.
Entry: Dr. A's Capital ₹10,000, Cr. B's Capital ₹10,000.
topic: Goodwill Treatment on Change in PSR
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This uses old ratio (2:1) for crediting B and C. But C's share didn't change (1/5 → 1/5). The adjustment is only between those who gained and those who sacrificed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing goodwill to all partners in old ratio instead of focusing on sacrifice/gain.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the debit and credit. A gained, so A pays (debit A). B sacrificed, so B receives (credit B).
elimination_hints.misconception: Debiting the sacrificer and crediting the gainer — the exact opposite of the correct treatment.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20,000 = 50,000 × 2/5 (A's new share). But the adjustment is based on the CHANGE in share (1/5), not the new share. 50,000 × 1/5 = ₹10,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the new share fraction instead of the gain/sacrifice fraction.

question_text: On change in PSR, if the Balance Sheet shows a debit balance of Profit and Loss Account (accumulated loss), it is:
explanation: A debit balance on the P&L Account represents accumulated losses. On change in PSR, this accumulated loss must be distributed (debited) to all partners' capital accounts in the OLD ratio. Entry: Dr. All Partners' Capital A/cs (old ratio), Cr. P&L A/c. This clears the loss before the new ratio takes effect, ensuring partners bear losses proportionate to the ratio under which they were incurred.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio applies after the change. The accumulated loss belongs to the old period and must be settled in the old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using new ratio for losses that occurred under the old arrangement.

elimination_hints.hint: Gaining ratio is used for goodwill compensation. Loss distribution uses the old PSR — a different concept entirely.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the gaining ratio to loss distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: Carrying forward would unfairly burden partners under the new ratio for losses incurred under the old arrangement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the need to settle old losses before applying new ratios.

question_text: R, S and T share profits in 3:2:1. They decide to share in 2:3:1. Goodwill of the firm is ₹1,80,000 and already appears in books at ₹60,000. If goodwill is to be raised at full value and then written off, the net effect on S's Capital Account is:
explanation:
Old: R=3/6, S=2/6, T=1/6. New: R=2/6, S=3/6, T=1/6.
Step 1: Write off old goodwill ₹60,000 in old ratio: R = 30,000 Dr, S = 20,000 Dr, T = 10,000 Dr.
Step 2: Raise new goodwill ₹1,80,000 in old ratio: R = 90,000 Cr, S = 60,000 Cr, T = 30,000 Cr.
Step 3: Write off new goodwill ₹1,80,000 in new ratio: R = 60,000 Dr, S = 90,000 Dr, T = 30,000 Dr.
Net on S = −20,000 + 60,000 − 90,000 = −₹50,000 (Debit).
Wait, let me reconsider. If goodwill is raised at full value (₹1,80,000) and already appears at ₹60,000, we raise the balance: Entry: Dr. Goodwill ₹1,20,000, Cr. Partners (old ratio). Then write off entire ₹1,80,000 in new ratio.
Alternatively: Write off old ₹60,000 in old, raise ₹1,80,000 in old, write off ₹1,80,000 in new.
S: −20,000 + 60,000 − 90,000 = −50,000 (Debit ₹50,000).
Or simpler method: Net S = Raised (old) − Written off old (old) − Written off new (new) = 60,000 − 20,000 − 90,000 = −50,000.
topic: Goodwill Treatment on Change in PSR
subtopic: Raise and write off with existing goodwill
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 Credit would mean S benefits. But S is the gainer (2/6 → 3/6). Gainers should have a net debit (they compensate the sacrificer).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not realizing that gainers end up with a net debit effect from goodwill adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹10,000 might result from ignoring one of the three steps. All three entries must be considered: write off old, raise new, write off new.
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing one of the goodwill adjustment steps.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹40,000 might come from a calculation error. Verify: old goodwill write-off = 20,000 Dr, new goodwill raised = 60,000 Cr, new goodwill written off = 90,000 Dr. Net = −50,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in one of the steps.

question_text: When a firm has a Workmen Compensation Reserve of ₹40,000 and there is an actual claim of ₹15,000 at the time of change in PSR, the treatment will be:
explanation:
Workmen Compensation Reserve = ₹40,000. Actual claim = ₹15,000.
Step 1: Pay the claim of ₹15,000. Entry: Dr. Workmen Compensation Reserve ₹15,000, Cr. Workmen Compensation Claim (Liability) ₹15,000.
Step 2: The balance (₹40,000 − ₹15,000 = ₹25,000) is no longer needed as a provision and is distributed to partners in the old ratio.
Entry: Dr. Workmen Compensation Reserve ₹25,000, Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs ₹25,000 (old ratio).
Total: Reserve of ₹40,000 used — ₹15,000 for claim, ₹25,000 distributed.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subject: accountancy

options.text: Entire ₹40,000 distributed to partners in old ratio
Only ₹15,000 paid as claim; ₹25,000 retained as reserve
₹15,000 used for claim; balance ₹25,000 distributed in old ratio

elimination_hints.hint: The firm has an actual claim of ₹15,000. This must be paid first from the reserve. Only the remaining ₹25,000 is distributable.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the actual claim and distributing the entire reserve.

elimination_hints.hint: Retaining the balance as reserve is incorrect during reconstitution. The excess reserve must be distributed because the new ratio will govern future provisions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Keeping unused reserves on the Balance Sheet during a change in PSR.

elimination_hints.hint: The balance ₹25,000 must be distributed in the OLD ratio since it was accumulated under the old arrangement. New ratio is not applicable here.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using new ratio for distributing reserves that were built under the old ratio.

question_text: In the Revaluation Account, an increase in the value of a liability is:
explanation: An increase in liability value means the firm owes MORE than previously recorded. This is a loss for the firm. In the Revaluation Account: an increase in liability is DEBITED (it represents a loss). Conversely, a decrease in liability is credited (gain). Remember: Revaluation Account — Asset increase = Credit (gain), Asset decrease = Debit (loss), Liability increase = Debit (loss), Liability decrease = Credit (gain).
subtopic: Treatment of liability changes in Revaluation Account
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debited to Profit and Loss Account

elimination_hints.hint: Credit in Revaluation Account represents a gain. An increase in liability is a LOSS (more to pay), not a gain. Gains are: asset increase or liability decrease.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the rules — thinking liability increase is favorable.

elimination_hints.hint: All changes in asset and liability values during reconstitution are recorded in the Revaluation Account. Nothing is omitted.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking only asset changes go to Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation entries go to the Revaluation Account, not the regular P&L Account. P&L deals with routine business, not reconstitution adjustments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the regular P&L Account for reconstitution-related revaluations.

question_text: P, Q and R share profits in 4:3:2. They decide to share in 2:4:3. A Memorandum Revaluation Account shows: Part I — Profit of ₹45,000. The amount transferred to Q's Capital Account as net effect of both parts is:
explanation:
Old: P=4/9, Q=3/9, R=2/9. New: P=2/9, Q=4/9, R=3/9.
Part I: Revaluation Profit ₹45,000 distributed in OLD ratio: Q = 45,000 × 3/9 = ₹15,000 (Credit).
Part II: Reversed, so Loss ₹45,000 distributed in NEW ratio: Q = 45,000 × 4/9 = ₹20,000 (Debit).
Net effect on Q = +15,000 − 20,000 = −₹5,000 (Debit ₹5,000).
subtopic: Memorandum Revaluation — net effect on partner
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹15,000 is only Part I's effect (credit in old ratio). You must also account for Part II (debit ₹20,000 in new ratio). Net = 15,000 − 20,000 = −5,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only Part I and ignoring Part II of the Memorandum Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20,000 is only Part II's effect (debit in new ratio). You must net it with Part I (credit ₹15,000). Net = −5,000, not −20,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only Part II and ignoring Part I.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,000 Credit would mean Q gains from the Memorandum Revaluation. But Q gained share (3/9 → 4/9), so Q should bear a net debit. Net = −5,000 (Debit).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the sign — getting the direction of net effect wrong.

question_text: An unrecorded asset discovered at the time of change in PSR is:
explanation: An unrecorded asset (one that exists but is not shown in the books) is a gain for the firm when discovered. It is recorded by debiting the Asset Account (to bring it into books) and crediting the Revaluation Account (as a gain). The gain is then distributed to partners in the old ratio through the Revaluation Account.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Ignored as it was not in the books

elimination_hints.hint: Debit to Revaluation = loss. But discovering a previously unrecorded asset is a GAIN, not a loss. It should be credited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating the discovery of an asset as a loss instead of a gain.

elimination_hints.hint: While the effect ultimately reaches partners' capitals, the proper accounting route is through the Revaluation Account, which then transfers to capitals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Bypassing the Revaluation Account and directly adjusting capital accounts.

elimination_hints.hint: Unrecorded items must be brought into books during reconstitution to ensure accurate valuation. They cannot be ignored.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking only existing book items are subject to revaluation.

question_text: J, K and L share profits in 1:1:1. They decide to share in 5:3:2. Investment Fluctuation Reserve of ₹30,000 exists. Investments (book value ₹1,00,000) are revalued at ₹90,000. The treatment of the Investment Fluctuation Reserve will be:
explanation:
Investment Fluctuation Reserve = ₹30,000. Investments decreased by ₹10,000 (1,00,000 → 90,000).
Step 1: The decrease of ₹10,000 is first absorbed by the reserve. Reserve used = ₹10,000.
Step 2: Balance of reserve = 30,000 − 10,000 = ₹20,000.
Step 3: This excess ₹20,000 is distributed to partners in the old ratio (1:1:1). Each partner gets ₹20,000/3 ≈ ₹6,667.
The reserve absorbs the investment loss first, and any excess is distributed.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subject: accountancy

options.text: ₹10,000 used for investment loss; ₹20,000 distributed in old ratio

elimination_hints.hint: The reserve exists to absorb investment value fluctuations. The ₹10,000 decrease must be absorbed first, leaving only ₹20,000 for distribution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the purpose of the reserve and distributing it all without absorbing the loss.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20,000 should not be retained — during reconstitution, all reserves must be settled. The excess after absorbing the loss is distributed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Retaining excess reserve instead of distributing during reconstitution.

elimination_hints.hint: The investment loss is only ₹10,000. Using ₹30,000 for a ₹10,000 loss makes no sense. The excess ₹20,000 goes to partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Over-applying the reserve beyond the actual loss amount.

question_text: W, X and Y share profits in 3:2:1. They decide to share in 1:2:3. Goodwill = ₹1,20,000 (not in books). General Reserve = ₹36,000. Revaluation loss = ₹18,000. The total net credit to Y's Capital Account for all adjustments combined is:
explanation:
Old: W=3/6, X=2/6, Y=1/6. New: W=1/6, X=2/6, Y=3/6.
1. Goodwill (single entry method): Gain/Sacrifice: W = 1/6−3/6 = −2/6 (sacrificed). X = 0. Y = 3/6−1/6 = +2/6 (gained).
Y compensates W. Amount = 1,20,000 × 2/6 = ₹40,000. Y debited ₹40,000, W credited ₹40,000.
2. General Reserve ₹36,000 in old ratio: Y = 36,000 × 1/6 = ₹6,000 (Cr).
3. Revaluation loss ₹18,000 in old ratio: Y = 18,000 × 1/6 = ₹3,000 (Dr).
Net on Y = −40,000 + 6,000 − 3,000 = −₹37,000 (Debit ₹37,000).
Wait, the question asks for net CREDIT. Y has a net debit of ₹37,000. Let me re-read... it asks total net credit, so if the result is debit, there's no net credit. Let me check the options.
subtopic: Total net effect of all adjustments
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,000 is only Y's share of General Reserve (credit). But Y also has: goodwill debit ₹40,000 and revaluation debit ₹3,000. Net = 6,000 − 40,000 − 3,000 = −37,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only one adjustment and ignoring the others.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹43,000 = 40,000 + 3,000. This adds the goodwill and revaluation debits but ignores the General Reserve credit of ₹6,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the General Reserve distribution which gives Y a credit.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,000 is only the revaluation loss share. Multiple adjustments must be netted together for the total effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only the revaluation adjustment.

question_text: The primary difference between a Revaluation Account and a Memorandum Revaluation Account is that:
explanation: The key difference: A Revaluation Account actually changes the book values of assets and liabilities in the Balance Sheet (real entries are passed). A Memorandum Revaluation Account does NOT change book values — it only adjusts capital accounts through two offsetting parts (Part I in old ratio, Part II reversed in new ratio). The assets and liabilities remain at their original book values in the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Revaluation Account is used during dissolution only
Memorandum Revaluation changes book values permanently
Revaluation Account does not affect partners' capital accounts
Revaluation Account changes book values; Memorandum does not

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation Account is used during reconstitution (admission, retirement, change in PSR), not dissolution. Dissolution uses Realisation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Revaluation Account with Realisation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: This is the exact opposite — Memorandum Revaluation Account keeps book values UNCHANGED. Regular Revaluation Account changes them.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the key characteristic of each account.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation Account DOES affect capital accounts — the net profit/loss is transferred to partners' capitals in the old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Revaluation Account is isolated from partner accounts.

question_text: In Revaluation Account, if a provision for doubtful debts is to be increased from ₹5,000 to ₹8,000, the Revaluation Account is:
explanation: Increasing provision for doubtful debts means the firm recognizes more potential bad debts — this is a loss. The increase = ₹8,000 − ₹5,000 = ₹3,000. This ₹3,000 is debited to Revaluation Account (loss) and credited to Provision for Doubtful Debts Account (to increase it).
subtopic: Provision for doubtful debts in revaluation
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Credit = gain. Increasing a provision (expecting more bad debts) is a LOSS, so it should be debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating an increase in provision as a gain instead of a loss.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,000 is the new total provision. Only the INCREASE of ₹3,000 (8,000 − 5,000) is recorded in the Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Recording the full new provision amount instead of the change.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,000 is the old provision amount, not the change. The Revaluation Account records the increase = ₹3,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the old provision value instead of the difference.

question_text:
Assertion (A): When goodwill is not to appear in the new Balance Sheet after change in PSR, it is raised in old ratio and immediately written off in new ratio.
Reason (R): Raising in old ratio credits all partners for goodwill they helped build, and writing off in new ratio charges it to partners in their new proportions.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — this is the standard 'raise and write off' method used when goodwill should not appear in the new Balance Sheet. The Reason is also TRUE — raising in old ratio correctly attributes the goodwill to partners who built it, and writing off in new ratio correctly allocates the cost to partners who will benefit going forward. R correctly explains A.
topic: Goodwill Treatment on Change in PSR
subtopic: Logic behind raise and write off method
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains the logic behind using old ratio for raising and new ratio for writing off. The causal connection is clear.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the direct explanatory link between R and A.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — the rationale of crediting builders (old ratio) and charging beneficiaries (new ratio) is the exact economic logic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the economic reasoning behind the dual-ratio approach.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — raise in old, write off in new is a standard accounting treatment taught in all textbooks.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the standard goodwill treatment for PSR changes.

question_text:
Assertion (A): In a Memorandum Revaluation Account, if Part I shows a loss, Part II will show a profit of the same amount.
Reason (R): Part II is the exact reversal of Part I, ensuring that actual book values of assets and liabilities remain unchanged.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — Part II is the exact reverse of Part I. If Part I shows a loss of ₹X, Part II shows a profit of ₹X (because it reverses every entry). The Reason is also TRUE — the reversal in Part II is done precisely to ensure book values don't change. The Reason correctly explains why Part II mirrors Part I — because reversal is needed to maintain unchanged book values.
subtopic: Part I and Part II relationship
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R explains exactly WHY Part II reverses Part I — to keep book values unchanged. This is the fundamental reason for the reversal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the reversal mechanism with the objective of maintaining book values.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — reversal is the core purpose of Part II. Without reversal, asset/liability values would change, defeating the purpose of a MEMORANDUM account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the fundamental purpose of the Memorandum Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — by definition, Part II reverses Part I. If Part I = Loss X, Part II = Profit X. They are mirror images.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Part I and Part II can show the same result (both loss or both profit).

question_text: If a partner's share in the new ratio is the same as in the old ratio, they neither sacrifice nor gain, and no goodwill adjustment is needed for that partner.
explanation: TRUE. If a partner's share remains unchanged (e.g., old = 2/6, new = 2/6), then New − Old = 0. They have neither gained nor sacrificed. No goodwill compensation is due to or from this partner. The goodwill adjustment only involves partners whose shares have changed.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: No change in share = no sacrifice, no gain. Goodwill adjustment is based on the CHANGE in share. Zero change = zero adjustment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking every partner must participate in goodwill adjustments regardless of whether their share changed.

question_text: The balance of the Revaluation Account after recording all changes in assets and liabilities is transferred to partners' capital accounts in the new ratio.
explanation: FALSE. The balance of the Revaluation Account is transferred to partners' capital accounts in the OLD ratio, not the new ratio. The revaluation relates to changes that occurred during the period of the old ratio. Using the new ratio would unfairly allocate gains/losses to partners who didn't contribute to them proportionately.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation profit/loss is transferred in the OLD ratio, not new. The changes in value occurred under the old arrangement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly using new ratio for pre-change adjustments.

question_text:
Match the following Balance Sheet items with their treatment during change in PSR:

Column I:
(i) General Reserve
(ii) P&L Account (Debit balance)
(iii) Workmen Compensation Reserve (no claim exists)
(iv) Advertisement Suspense Account

Column II:
(P) Distributed (debited) to partners in old ratio as it is a loss
(Q) Distributed (credited) to partners in old ratio as it is accumulated profit
(R) Distributed (credited) to partners in old ratio — full amount is free reserve
(S) Distributed (debited) to partners in old ratio as it is a deferred expense
explanation: (i) General Reserve → (Q): Accumulated profit distributed to partners (credited) in old ratio. (ii) P&L Debit balance → (P): Accumulated loss distributed to partners (debited) in old ratio. (iii) Workmen Compensation Reserve (no claim) → (R): Since no claim exists, the entire amount is free and distributed to partners (credited) in old ratio. (iv) Advertisement Suspense → (S): It is a deferred revenue expenditure (fictitious asset), distributed to partners (debited) in old ratio to write it off.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subtopic: Different Balance Sheet items during PSR change
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This matches P&L debit (ii) with (S) deferred expense. P&L debit is accumulated loss (P), not a deferred expense. Advertisement Suspense is the deferred expense (S).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing accumulated loss with deferred revenue expenditure.

elimination_hints.hint: This matches General Reserve (i) with (R) free reserve and WCR (iii) with (Q) accumulated profit. While both are credits, General Reserve is Q (accumulated profit) and WCR with no claim is R (free reserve).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the specific descriptions of General Reserve and Workmen Compensation Reserve.

elimination_hints.hint: This completely reverses the credits and debits — treating reserves as losses and losses as reserves.
elimination_hints.misconception: Fundamentally confusing credit items (reserves) with debit items (losses).

question_text: When profit-sharing ratio changes, the existing goodwill shown in the Balance Sheet must be written off by debiting partners' capital accounts in the _______ ratio.
explanation: Existing goodwill must be written off in the OLD profit-sharing ratio. This is because the goodwill was built up during the period when partners shared profits in the old ratio. Each partner's share of the write-off should correspond to their share under the old arrangement.
topic: Goodwill Treatment on Change in PSR
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio applies after the change. Existing goodwill was created under the old arrangement and must be written off in the OLD ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using new ratio for items belonging to the old period.

elimination_hints.hint: Gaining ratio is used for goodwill compensation between gainers and sacrificers. Writing off existing goodwill uses the old PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing goodwill write-off with goodwill compensation.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital ratio is based on capital contributions, not on profit-sharing. Goodwill write-off uses the old PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using capital ratio instead of profit-sharing ratio.

question_text:
A, B and C share profits in 2:2:1. They agree to share in 3:1:1 from next year. The following information is given:
- Goodwill valued at ₹50,000 (not in books)
- General Reserve: ₹25,000
- P&L A/c (Cr.): ₹10,000
- Revaluation: Land +₹30,000, Machinery −₹15,000, Creditors overstated by ₹5,000

What is the net effect on B's Capital Account?
explanation:
Old: A=2/5, B=2/5, C=1/5. New: A=3/5, B=1/5, C=1/5.
1. Goodwill (single entry): Gain/Sacrifice: A = 3/5−2/5 = +1/5 (gained). B = 1/5−2/5 = −1/5 (sacrificed). C = 1/5−1/5 = 0.
A compensates B: 50,000 × 1/5 = ₹10,000. B credited ₹10,000.
2. General Reserve ₹25,000 in old ratio: B = 25,000 × 2/5 = ₹10,000 (Cr).
3. P&L (Cr.) ₹10,000 in old ratio: B = 10,000 × 2/5 = ₹4,000 (Cr).
4. Revaluation: Credits: Land +30,000 + Creditors −5,000 (overstated reduced = gain) = ₹35,000. Debits: Machinery −15,000. Net profit = ₹20,000. B's share (old ratio) = 20,000 × 2/5 = ₹8,000 (Cr).
Net on B = +10,000 + 10,000 + 4,000 + 8,000 = ₹32,000 Credit.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹22,000 = 10,000 + 4,000 + 8,000. This includes reserve, P&L and revaluation but misses the goodwill compensation of ₹10,000 that B receives as the sacrificing partner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the goodwill adjustment in the total calculation.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹24,000 = 10,000 + 10,000 + 4,000. This includes goodwill, reserve and P&L but misses the revaluation profit share of ₹8,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to include revaluation profit distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹18,000 might result from miscalculating one or more adjustments. All four items must be summed: goodwill (10k) + reserve (10k) + P&L (4k) + revaluation (8k) = 32k.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error or missing multiple adjustment components.

question_text:
Arrange the following in the correct order for preparing the Memorandum Revaluation Account:
(i) Reverse all revaluation entries and distribute in new ratio (Part II)
(ii) Calculate net profit or loss on revaluation
(iii) Record increases and decreases in assets and liabilities
(iv) Distribute the net revaluation profit/loss in old ratio (Part I)
(v) Verify that assets and liabilities show at original book values
explanation: Correct order: (1) Record changes in assets and liabilities (iii) — this is the starting point of Part I. (2) Calculate net profit/loss on revaluation (ii) — sum the debits and credits. (3) Distribute in old ratio — Part I (iv). (4) Reverse all entries and distribute in new ratio — Part II (i). (5) Verify book values unchanged (v) — final check. Order: (iii) → (ii) → (iv) → (i) → (v).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This calculates net profit (ii) before recording the changes (iii). You can't calculate the net without first recording the individual changes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Computing the result before recording the underlying entries.

elimination_hints.hint: This distributes in old ratio (iv) before calculating net profit (ii). You must know the net result before distributing it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing before calculating what needs to be distributed.

elimination_hints.hint: This starts with Part II reversal (i) before Part I is even prepared. Part I (original revaluation) must come first, then Part II (reversal).
elimination_hints.misconception: Starting with the reversal before the original entries exist.
